items.passage: Hydrothermal vents are cracks on the ocean floor where superheated, mineral-rich water rises from below the crust. Around these openings live strange animals that can survive in deep darkness without any light at all. Tube worms and pale crabs depend on bacteria that turn toxic chemicals into useful energy. This unusual process, called chemosynthesis, replaces the photosynthesis that supports most life above. Scientists study these communities to learn how organisms might endure such harsh and hostile conditions.

Microplastics are tiny fragments of plastic, smaller than a grain of rice, that now contaminate nearly every ocean. They form when larger items break apart slowly under sunlight, waves, and the constant grinding of sand. Marine animals frequently mistake these particles for food and swallow them along with their normal prey. The harmful substances can then accumulate in tissues and pass upward through the entire food chain. Researchers warn that this pollution may eventually threaten human health as well.

Children acquire language with remarkable speed during the first few years of their early lives. Long before formal schooling begins, they absorb the sounds and patterns of speech from those around them. By listening attentively, infants gradually learn to connect spoken words with the objects they represent. This steady progress depends heavily on frequent interaction with attentive parents and other caregivers. Psychologists believe that such early exposure shapes the developing brain in lasting and profound ways.

items.subtopic: the building of ancient Egyptian pyramids
items.passage: The ancient Egyptians built enormous stone pyramids to serve as elaborate tombs for their powerful kings. Thousands of workers cut, hauled, and stacked the heavy blocks under the hot desert sun. Careful planning let the builders align each side neatly with the four main compass directions. Inside, narrow passages led to hidden rooms that were filled with treasures for the afterlife. Modern engineers still admire the skill and patience that these vast monuments clearly required.

The rigid outer shell of the Earth is broken into several enormous moving plates. Driven by slow currents deep within the hot mantle, these plates drift steadily across the planet. Where two of them collide, the crust may crumple upward and raise towering mountain ranges. Elsewhere, plates pull apart and allow molten rock to rise and form fresh ocean floor. Geologists track this gradual motion to predict earthquakes and explain the shifting shape of continents.

Exoplanets are distant worlds that orbit stars far beyond the boundaries of our own solar system. Astronomers cannot usually see them directly because the brilliant glare of their stars hides them. Instead, scientists watch for the tiny dip in brightness that occurs when a planet crosses its star. This clever method has already revealed thousands of strange and surprisingly varied new worlds. Some of them lie within the zone where liquid water could possibly exist.
